summary:
The beginning of the Maya Classic Period (c. 250–900 AD) marked the florescence of one of the most sophisticated civilizations in pre-Columbian America — characterized by the proliferation of large urban centers with monumental stone architecture, the widespread use of the Long Count calendar (the most precise calendar in the ancient world), the development of hieroglyphic writing (the only fully developed writing system in the pre-Columbian Americas), sophisticated astronomical observation, and complex political hierarchies of city-states ruled by divine kings. The period opened with the establishment of clear written royal dynasties at centers like Tikal, Uaxactun, and Copan.

The Classic Maya world was a constellation of competing and cooperating city-states rather than a unified empire — interconnected through warfare, diplomacy, royal marriages, and trade. The two dominant political poles were the Tikal-affiliated and Calakmul-affiliated super-states, between which smaller polities were pulled into shifting alliance networks. Maya intellectual achievements during this period included precise calculations of the Venus cycle (accurate to within two hours over 500 years), architectural wonders aligned to astronomical events, and elaborate written histories carved in stone stelae that recorded royal genealogies and conquests. [[event:2069|Teotihuacan's]] powerful influence on the early Classic Maya — including a possible intervention or alliance at Tikal around 378 AD — is documented in the archaeological and hieroglyphic record.
